message.content:
You are analyzing how three browser apps (DAM, CRM, PM/PIM) relate to a shared Supabase database, for a PLM-backend migration plan. These apps are ALREADY (or being) migrated onto the shared Supabase project qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai. We need to understand their data-access pattern as the model the PLM migration may follow.

Clone shallow (default branch `main`); do NOT modify or commit:
  git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/u2giants/popdam3.git C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popdam3
  git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/u2giants/popcrm-web.git C:/repos/.dflow-audit/popcrm-web
  git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/u2giants/poppim-web.git C:/repos/.dflow-audit/poppim-web

For EACH of the three, read the README/.md files and the Supabase integration layer (src/lib/supabase.*, src/integrations/supabase/*, api files). Don't read everything — focus on the data-access architecture.

Report ONE structured markdown summary with these sections:
1. **Per-app snapshot** (one short block each for popdam3, popcrm-web, poppim-web): framework, is it a static browser SPA or does it have a server backend?, does it use @supabase/supabase-js directly from the browser, which schemas/tables/views/RPCs it reads, auth method (Supabase Auth? Entra? anon key?).
2. **Direct-Supabase vs API-layer** — do these apps hit base tables directly, or go through `api.*` views/RPCs? Do any have server-side backends or Edge Functions, or is it pure browser-to-Supabase?
3. **Auth model** — how do these apps authenticate to Supabase (Supabase Auth, JWT, anon+RLS)? How do roles/claims work?
4. **RLS reliance** — evidence of how much they depend on RLS for security (since browser holds keys).
5. **Shared-DB coordination** — any references to the shared-db repo, AGENTS.md rules, migration ledger, preview branch workflow.
6. **Implications for PLM** — given these are browser-direct-to-Supabase apps, what does that imply about whether PLM (which has heavy Node backends + Azure JWT auth) should follow the same pattern or keep its backend? Note tensions.

Tight and factual, cite file paths.

content.content:
1	# Architecture
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	PopDAM3 is a hybrid cloud/on-premises Digital Asset Manager for licensed consumer-product art. A React/Vite frontend (served via nginx on a Coolify-managed VPS) connects to a Supabase PostgreSQL database and twelve Deno edge functions (project ID `qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai`). A Node.js bridge agent running in Docker on a Synology NAS scans the filesystem, generates thumbnails, and pushes them to DigitalOcean Spaces; a separate Windows render agent handles Illustrator files. A persistent Railway cloud worker runs AI tagging, ERP enrichment, and style group rebuilds by polling a job queue stored in the `admin_config` table. The same Docker image serves both PopDAM (`dam.designflow.app`) and PopSG (`sg.designflow.app`); the active mode is determined at runtime by hostname.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Components
10	
11	### Frontend (React/Vite)
12	
13	Located in `src/`. Built with Vite 5, React 18, Tailwind CSS, Shadcn/UI, and TanStack Query 5. Served from an `nginx:1.27-alpine` container (GHCR image `ghcr.io/u2giants/popdam-frontend`) managed by Coolify (app UUID `qxj8a0j3tpa9lq4q5rs6pezy`) on VPS `178.156.180.212`. Traefik terminates TLS and routes both production hostnames to the same container.
14	
15	**Dual-mode design:** `src/lib/app-mode.ts` runs mode detection once at module load. Priority order: (1) `?mode=popsg` or `?mode=popdam` query param, persisted to `sessionStorage`; (2) existing `sessionStorage` value; (3) hostname (`sg.*` or `popsg.*` prefix → PopSG; everything else → PopDAM). Exports `APP_MODE`, `IS_POPSG`, `IS_POPDAM`, and `CURRENT_APP`. The Supabase URL and anon key (`qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai`) are hardcoded in this file — the Lovable platform overwrites `.env` on every deploy, so environment variables cannot be used. PopSG-specific pages live in `src/pages/popsg/`; the router in `src/App.tsx` guards them with `IS_POPSG`. PopDAM-only tables (`assets`, `style_groups`, `erp_items_current`) are never queried in PopSG mode.
16	
17	**Key areas:**
18	- `src/components/library/` — asset grid, detail panel, filter sidebar (PopDAM only)
19	- `src/components/settings/` — admin UI tabs: bulk ops, ERP, agents, diagnostics
20	- `src/integrations/supabase/types.ts` — auto-generated by `deploy-supabase.yml` on every deploy; do not edit manually
21	
22	### Supabase (DB + Edge Functions)
23	
24	**Project ID:** `qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai` (host: `qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai.supabase.co`)
25	
26	PostgreSQL with extensions `pg_cron`, `pg_net`, `pg_trgm`, `pgcrypto`, `pgsodium`/Vault. Schema changes are managed via timestamped migration files in `supabase/migrations/` applied through `supabase db push` in CI (`deploy-supabase.yml`). Migration filename timestamps must exactly match the timestamp Supabase records when `apply_migration` MCP is called.
27	
28	Twelve deployed Deno edge functions (see Key Edge Functions section). Functions that require CORS preflight have `verify_jwt = false` in `supabase/config.toml`; JWT verification is done inside the function body. Shared code lives in `supabase/functions/_shared/`.
29	
30	### Bridge Agent
31	
32	Located in `apps/bridge-agent/`. A TypeScript/Node.js process packaged as a Docker image (`ghcr.io/u2giants/popdam-bridge`, tag `:stable` for production). Runs in Docker on the Synology NAS via a reference compose file in `deploy/synology/`.
33	
34	**What it does:** registers with `agent-api` using a one-time pairing code, then enters a continuous loop. Receives commands via Supabase Realtime (instant) or heartbeat polling (fallback every 30 s). On a scan command: validates roots → walks the NAS filesystem → computes quick hash (first 64 KB + last 64 KB + file size) → generates thumbnails using MuPDF and Sharp → uploads thumbnails to DigitalOcean Spaces → calls `agent-api` to ingest metadata into Supabase. Also handles PDF text sampling, AI sentinel file detection, PopSG style guide crawls, and self-update.
35	
36	**Communication:** outbound HTTPS only. The cloud never initiates connections to the NAS. Commands are delivered via the heartbeat response payload or Supabase Realtime channel.
37	
38	**Multi-tenant support:** `tenant-supervisor.ts` spawns one child process per tenant when the `TENANTS` env var is set. In the standard single-tenant install this is unused.
39	
40	**Key env vars:** `SUPABASE_URL`, `AGENT_KEY`, `SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` (for Realtime), `NAS_CONTAINER_MOUNT_ROOT`, `SCAN_ROOTS`, `DO_SPACES_*`, `POPDAM_CONTAINER_NAME`.
41	
42	### Worker
43	
44	Located in `apps/worker/`. A persistent Node.js process deployed on Railway (auto-deploys from every push to `main`). Uses a service-role Supabase client (`SUPABASE_URL` + `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY`).
45	
46	**What it does:** polls `admin_config.BULK_OPERATIONS` every 1 s (configurable). Each tick reads the operations JSON blob, detects stale locks, auto-resumes interrupted ops, promotes queued ops into empty lanes with conflict isolation, dispatches batch handlers, and persists state back via the `update_bulk_operation` RPC. There is no HTTP endpoint; all triggering is done by writing to `admin_config.BULK_OPERATIONS` from the frontend or admin API.
47	
48	**Handlers:** `ai-tagging.ts` (AI tag operations), `erp.ts` (ERP enrichment + AI category classification), `style-groups.ts` (rebuild, reconcile, cleanup), `relink-orphaned.ts`, `tag-propagation.ts`.
49	
50	**Lane isolation:** ops are assigned lanes (`ai-tagging`, `style-groups`, `erp`, `metadata`). Cross-lane conflicts are enforced (e.g., `ai-tag-*` and `rebuild-style-groups` cannot run concurrently). Kill switches stop a lane after 20 identical errors or 80%+ failure rate over 50+ items.
51	
52	### popdam-helper
53	
54	Located in `apps/popdam-helper/`. An Electron desktop application (built with electron-vite, React 18) distributed for Windows (x64) and macOS (arm64 + x64) via GitHub Releases (`popdam-helper-latest` tag), built by `publish-popdam-helper.yml`.
55	
56	**What it does:** provides a GUI for the checkout/checkin workflow. Users check out assets from the DAM to local disk, edit them, then check them back in via Synology WebDAV upload. Watches local files with `chokidar`. Runs a local HTTP server on port 47380 (`GET /status`, `GET /browse?path=X`) for fast directory browsing from the web UI. Credentials (Supabase session + Synology credentials) stored in OS keychain via `safeStorage`. Upload queue persisted to `userData/upload-queue.json` with exponential-backoff retry. Auto-update is wired via `electron-updater` but not yet active — blocked on code signing certificates.
57	
58	---
59	
60	## Data Flow
61	
62	### 1. Asset Ingestion
63	
64	1. Admin triggers a scan from the web UI (Settings → Agents) or the bridge agent receives a `SCAN_REQUEST` from `admin_config`.
65	2. `admin-api` writes the command; the bridge agent picks it up on the next heartbeat or via Supabase Realtime.
66	3. Bridge agent walks the NAS filesystem, collects the scan candidates, and calls `check-changed` across the scan before ingesting files. Unchanged rows seed a per-scan `(quick_hash, filename)` identity set; new/changed files are hashed (first/last 64 KB + size). If the same identity is seen more than once in a scan, later copies bypass hash move detection and settle as path-specific asset rows.
67	4. For new/changed files, the bridge agent generates a thumbnail (MuPDF for PDF, Sharp for images, queues to Windows Agent for `.ai`), uploads it to DigitalOcean Spaces bucket `popdam` (CDN: `cdn.designflow.app`), and records the `thumbnail_url`.
68	5. Bridge agent calls `POST /agent/ingest` on `agent-api` for each processed file, bounded by the agent's thumbnail/ingest concurrency. `agent-api` upserts rows into `assets`, setting `relative_path` (canonical POSIX, no leading slash), `quick_hash`, `modified_at` (filesystem mtime, never server time), `thumbnail_url`, and derived metadata. Hash move detection is used only when the incoming path has no existing row, the candidate is unique by `(quick_hash, filename)`, and the bridge did not mark the file as a duplicate seen in the current scan.
69	6. The `trg_compute_primary_sort_tier` trigger assigns a cover tier on every insert/update. The `trg_sync_primary_on_thumbnail` trigger (fires on INSERT and UPDATE) updates `style_groups.primary_asset_id` when a thumbnail is set.
70	7. Bridge agent calls `POST /agent/scan-progress` throughout and reports completion.
71	
72	### 2. ERP Sync
73	
74	1. `erp-sync` edge function is triggered from Settings → ERP Enrichment (full or incremental sync).
75	2. Fetches from `https://api.item.designflow.app/lib/getApiAllItems` (incremental: watermark from `ERP_LAST_SYNC_DATE`; full: all items).
76	3. Normalizes field values through `supabase/functions/_shared/mg-codes.ts` (reverse-lookup: API description → single-char MG code). Unknown descriptions are stored as `null` code + raw value in `raw_mg_fields` jsonb.
77	4. Upserts rows into `erp_items_current` (one row per `external_id`), snapshots to `erp_items_raw`, records run metadata in `erp_sync_runs`.
78	5. The Railway worker's `erp-enrichment` handler runs separately: reads `erp_items_current` in batches of 100, matches style numbers to `style_groups`/`assets`, writes ERP metadata. Guard: only uses `mg_category` to set `product_category` when `erp_updated_at >= 2025-05-10` (pre-cutoff items fall through to AI classification).
79	
80	### 3. Sibling Scan
81	
82	1. Frontend calls `admin-api` route `list-sibling-images` or `ingest-sibling-images`.
83	2. Admin-api writes a `sibling_scan_request_*` row to `admin_config`.
84	3. Bridge agent claims the request via `agent-api` route `claim-sibling-scan`, walks the specified folder looking for sibling JPG/PNG/eligible PDF files alongside existing assets, and reports results through `complete-sibling-scan`.
85	4. `claimed` is a lease. Admin-api expires stale claimed rows after 10 minutes, and agent-api allows the Bridge Agent to reclaim stale claims. Do not remove this; otherwise a Bridge Agent restart or exception can leave the UI waiting forever for the same folder.
86	5. Admin-api handler (`sibling-scan-handlers.ts`) ingests confirmed siblings into `assets`.
87	
88	### 4. AI Classification
89	
90	1. Admin triggers "Classify Now" from Settings → ERP Enrichment → calls `admin-api` route `classify-erp-categories` → writes `erp-classify` op to `admin_config.BULK_OPERATIONS`.
91	2. Railway worker's `erp-classify` handler reads `erp_items_current` records lacking `product_category`, batches them, and calls OpenRouter (model from `admin_config.AI_TASK_MODELS.text_classification`, default `anthropic/claude-3.5-haiku`, cached 60 s).
92	3. AI returns category + confidence. Items with confidence ≥ 0.65 get status `auto_applied`; below threshold get `pending`.
93	4. Results inserted into `product_category_predictions`.
94	5. Human reviews pending predictions in ERP Items Browser or Review Queue. Approved predictions are pushed to `style_groups.product_category` and `assets.product_category` via "Apply ERP Enrichment" (`erp-enrichment` worker op).
95	
96	---
97	
98	## Key Database Tables
99	
100	| Table | Purpose |
101	|-------|---------|
102	| `assets` | One row per indexed file. Key columns: `relative_path` (POSIX, no leading slash), `quick_hash`, `modified_at` (filesystem mtime, NOT NULL), `thumbnail_url`, `style_group_id`, `primary_sort_tier`, `product_category`, `ai_tagged_at` |
103	| `style_groups` | One row per SKU+folder. Key columns: `sku`, `folder_path`, `primary_asset_id`, `asset_count`, `designer_conflict`, `product_category`, `erp_updated_at` |
104	| `asset_tags` | Normalized tag rows; `source` is `'manual'` or `'ai'` |
105	| `asset_characters` | Character associations for assets |
106	| `asset_path_history` | Records path changes for move detection |
107	| `erp_items_current` | Latest normalized ERP row per `external_id`; `mg01_code`–`mg06_code`, `raw_mg_fields` jsonb |
108	| `erp_items_raw` | Immutable ERP API response snapshots |
109	| `erp_sync_runs` | ERP sync run metadata and status |
110	| `product_category_predictions` | AI classification proposals; statuses: `pending`, `auto_applied`, `approved`, `rejected`, `unclassifiable` |
111	| `admin_config` | Key/value config and lightweight job queue. Keys include `BULK_OPERATIONS`, `SCAN_REQUEST`, `SPACES_CONFIG`, `AI_TASK_MODELS`, `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`, `BRIDGE_LATEST_BUILD` |
112	| `agent_registrations` | Registered agents; hashed keys, last heartbeat timestamp |
113	| `agent_pairings` | One-time pairing codes (15-minute TTL) |
114	| `profiles` | User profiles |
115	| `user_roles` | Role assignments |
116	| `invitations` | Pending email invitations |
117	| `app_access` | Controls which app(s) a user can access (`popdam` or `styleguides`) |
118	| `render_queue` | Pending Windows render agent jobs |
119	| `pdf_text_samples` | Extracted text from PDF and AI files (for sentinel detection and AI tagging context) |
120	| `ai_sentinel_cleanup_log` | Log of sentinel `.ai` file cleanup operations (temporary — delete when reconciliation is complete) |
121	| `scanner_ai_ignores` | Files permanently ignored by the scanner (e.g., blank-render AI files); includes `snoozed_until` |
122	| `style_guide_files` | PopSG crawled files; `licensor_name`, `is_active`, `thumbnail_url` |
123	| `style_guide_folders` | DISTINCT folder view for PopSG sidebar |
124	| `style_guide_crawl_runs` | PopSG crawl run metadata |
125	| `style_guide_render_queue` | PopSG Windows render jobs |
126	| `helper_devices` | Registered popdam-helper desktop devices |
127	| `helper_tokens` | Auth tokens for helper app sessions |
128	| `asset_checkouts` | Active and historical checkout records |
129	
130	---
131	
132	## Key Edge Functions
133	
134	| Function | Purpose |
135	|----------|---------|
136	| `admin-api` | Admin control plane (~1,300 lines). Routes for scan management, render queue, agent management, ERP enrichment, tag propagation, PDF backfill, diagnostics, TIFF pipeline, sibling scan, user/invitation management. Auth: user JWT + admin role, or service role key as Bearer. |
137	| `agent-api` | Bridge and Windows agent communication (~4,100 lines). Routes include `heartbeat`, `ingest`, `check-changed`, `scan-progress`, checkpoint routes, render queue routes, TIFF/hygiene/sibling-scan routes, PDF backfill/text-sample routes, style-guide crawl, and check-in verification. Auth: `x-agent-key` header. |
138	| `helper-api` | Checkout/checkin workflow for the Electron desktop helper. Auth: user JWT. |
139	| `erp-sync` | Fetches from the DesignFlow ERP API and upserts into `erp_items_current` / `erp_items_raw`. |
140	| `ai-tag` | Legacy AI tagging; currently used mainly for PDF text extraction via Google Gemini direct. |
141	| `authenticate-with-authentik` | Legacy Authentik SSO PKCE flow handler; validates via JWKS, exchanges for Supabase session. Hidden from the login page while Microsoft/Azure is primary. |
142	| `bulk-job-runner` | No-op stub. Returns `{ ok: true, message: "replaced by railway worker" }`. The pg_cron schedule that called this was removed in migration `20260322000000`. |
143	| `sync-external` | Syncs licensors, properties, and characters from external APIs. |
144	| `send-invite-email` | Sends invitation emails via Brevo transactional email. |
145	| `export-sql-dump` | Chunked SQL INSERT export for table data. |
146	| `export-table` | CSV/JSON table data export. |
147	| `export-thumbnail-manifest` | Exports a CSV of asset thumbnail URLs. |
148	| `verify-app-access` | Checks whether a user has access to a given app (`popdam` or `styleguides`). |
149	
150	---
151	
152	## Dual-Mode Design
153	
154	Both `dam.designflow.app` (PopDAM) and `sg.designflow.app` (PopSG) are served by the same Docker container and the same Supabase project (`qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai`). Traefik routes both hostnames to the `popdam-frontend` container (Coolify UUID `qxj8a0j3tpa9lq4q5rs6pezy`). The `sg.designflow.app` routing is configured via a Traefik file provider at `/data/coolify/proxy/dynamic/popdam-sg.yml` on the VPS host, referencing the Traefik service name `https-0-qxj8a0j3tpa9lq4q5rs6pezy@docker`.
155	
156	At runtime, `src/lib/app-mode.ts` detects the active mode from the hostname and sets `IS_POPSG` / `IS_POPDAM`. Mode differences are entirely in the UI layer:
157	
158	- **PopDAM** (`dam.designflow.app`): full DAM with SKU-based asset library, style groups, ERP integration, AI tagging, filter sidebar, bulk operations.
159	- **PopSG** (`sg.designflow.app`): licensor style guide library with folder-based browsing. Uses `style_guide_files` and `style_guide_folders` instead of `assets` and `style_groups`. No SKUs, no ERP, no PopDAM filter sidebar.
160	
161	PopSG-specific pages are in `src/pages/popsg/`. The router in `src/App.tsx` guards them with `IS_POPSG`. To preview PopSG locally: add `?mode=popsg` to the URL (persisted in `sessionStorage` for the tab).
162	
163	The `supabase-popsg/` directory in the repo root is dead code from an earlier architecture where PopSG had its own Supabase project. It is never deployed from.
164	
165	---
166	
167	## admin_config Pattern
168	
169	`admin_config` is a key/value table in Supabase that serves two distinct roles:
170	
171	**1. Configuration store.** Keys like `SPACES_CONFIG`, `AI_TASK_MODELS`, `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`, `POPDAM_MOUNT_PATH`, `POPSG_MOUNT_PATH`, `AGENT_API_KEY` hold runtime configuration. The bridge agent receives a full config sync payload on each heartbeat response (secrets are never included in the heartbeat — `DO_SPACES_KEY` and `DO_SPACES_SECRET` stay in the agent's local `.env`).
172	
173	**2. Lightweight job queue.** The key `BULK_OPERATIONS` holds a JSON object keyed by operation name, each with a status (`queued`, `running`, `interrupted`, `completed`, `failed`, `user_stop`), cursor, and progress counters. The Railway worker polls this key every 1 s. The frontend or `admin-api` writes a `queued` entry to trigger a job; the worker picks it up on the next poll, sets it to `running`, processes batches, and writes progress back after each batch. State transitions are atomic via the `update_bulk_operation` RPC with an optimistic-lock guard (`p_only_if_status`). The `SCAN_REQUEST` and `STYLE_GUIDE_CRAWL_REQUEST` keys work similarly for triggering agent operations.
174	
175	---
176	
177	## External Services
178	
179	| Service | Role |
180	|---------|------|
181	| **Supabase** (`qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai`) | PostgreSQL database, Auth, Realtime, Edge Functions runtime (Deno). Single project for both PopDAM and PopSG. |
182	| **DigitalOcean Spaces** | S3-compatible object storage. Bucket `popdam`, region `nyc3`. CDN at `cdn.designflow.app`. Stores all asset thumbnails. Path format: `thumbnails/{asset_id}.jpg`. |
183	| **OpenRouter / Anthropic** | AI inference. OpenRouter (`OPENROUTER_API_KEY`) is the preferred provider; `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` and `GOOGLE_AI_API_KEY` are fallbacks. Used by the Railway worker for ERP category classification and AI asset tagging. The bridge agent's PDF text extraction uses a vision model via OpenRouter as a last-resort fallback after MuPDF + OCR. |
184	| **Coolify** | Container orchestration on VPS `178.156.180.212`. Owns the `popdam-frontend` container lifecycle, runtime env vars, domain bindings, health checks, and restart policy. CI triggers deploys via the Coolify REST API (`POST /api/v1/deploy?uuid=qxj8a0j3tpa9lq4q5rs6pezy`). |
185	| **Railway** | Hosts the cloud worker (`apps/worker/`). Auto-deploys from every push to `main`. Worker connects to Supabase directly using a service role key. |
186	| **DesignFlow ERP API** | `https://api.item.designflow.app/lib/getApiAllItems`. Source of ERP item data synced into `erp_items_current`. |
187	| **Brevo** | Transactional email for invitation emails, via the `send-invite-email` edge function. |
188	| **Authentik** | Legacy company SSO provider at `auth.designflow.app`. PKCE flow handled by `authenticate-with-authentik` edge function; login button hidden while Microsoft/Azure is primary. |
189	| **GitHub Container Registry (GHCR)** | Stores Docker images: `ghcr.io/u2giants/popdam-frontend` (`:latest`, `:<sha>`) and `ghcr.io/u2giants/popdam-bridge` (`:stable`, `:latest`, `:v{version}`). |
190	| **Synology NAS** | On-premises file storage. Source of all design assets. Accessed only by the bridge agent running in Docker on the NAS itself; never accessed directly by the cloud. |
191	
192	---
193	
194	## Communication Model (no inbound NAS networking)
195	
196	**Hard rule:** the cloud backend never reaches into the NAS by IP. All NAS↔cloud traffic is **outbound-only** from the agents:
197	
198	- The cloud sets work flags / queues work in the DB (`admin_config` keys, `render_queue`, etc.).
199	- The Bridge Agent and Windows Agent poll outward (HTTPS) to claim work and report progress; they also receive commands instantly via a Supabase Realtime channel.
200	- The cloud never initiates a connection to the NAS or the Windows VM.
201	
202	Tailscale may be used for human remote access to NAS files (Synology Drive) and for WFH Brazil's Seafile→Synology fallback, but it is **not** required for the core agent-to-cloud workflow.
203	
204	---
205	
206	## API Boundaries
207	
208	Three separate edge-function surfaces, never mixed:
209	
210	### `agent-api` (`verify_jwt = false`)
211	- Auth: `x-agent-key` header (SHA-256 hashed, compared against `agent_registrations.agent_key_hash`).
212	- Routes: ingest/update/move, scan progress, heartbeat, claim/complete render + PDF-backfill + sibling-scan + checkin-verification jobs, style-guide crawl.
213	
214	### `admin-api` (`verify_jwt = false` — JWT verified inside the function)
215	- Auth: user JWT + admin role check **or** the Supabase service-role key as Bearer (maps to `userId: "system"`, used by the Railway worker for server-to-server calls).
216	- The `run-query` action restricts queries to `SELECT` only.
217	
218	### `helper-api` (`verify_jwt = true` — standard user JWT)
219	- Auth: user JWT — the Helper authenticates as the logged-in user.
220	- Routes: device registration, token generation, checkout/check-in lifecycle, heartbeat.
221	
222	### Railway worker (server process, no HTTP listener)
223	- Auth: `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` env var → service-role Supabase client (bypasses RLS). For operations it can't do via RPC, it calls `admin-api` with the service-role key as Bearer.
224	
225	---
226	
227	## Security Rules
228	
229	- No endpoint accepts arbitrary shell commands or raw command strings.
230	- Agent keys: store only hashes; the raw key is shown once on creation and never returned again.
231	- All edge functions use `corsServe()` from `supabase/functions/_shared/http.ts`, which validates request `Origin` against an allowlist (`*.designflow.app`, `*.lovable.app`, localhost).
232	- `verify_jwt = false` on `admin-api`/`agent-api` is intentional (CORS preflight carries no auth header) — verification happens inside the function body. See `docs/KNOWN_QUIRKS.md` #4.
233	
234	---
235	
236	## Golden Rule: file-date preservation
237	
238	The Bridge Agent must **never** modify file timestamps (mtime/birthtime) on source art. The NAS volume mount should be `:ro` (read-only) wherever possible. Full rule: `docs/PROJECT_BIBLE.md` §15.

1	# Authentication
2	
3	PopDAM uses Supabase Auth for session management. Login is available through Microsoft/Azure, Google, and email/password. A legacy Authentik path still exists in code but is hidden from the login page. All paths produce the same kind of Supabase JWT that the rest of the app uses without modification.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Path 1 — Microsoft OAuth / Azure AD
8	
9	**As of 2026-06-08, this is the active company SSO path for internal users.**
10	
11	Users sign in with their Microsoft / Azure AD account via Supabase's built-in Azure provider. New Azure users bypass invitation rows in `handle_new_user()` and are auto-provisioned with the `user` role and `popdam` app access. Google and email/password still require invitations.
12	
13	### Relevant files
14	
15	| File | Purpose |
16	|------|---------|
17	| `src/pages/LoginPage.tsx` | Renders the "Continue with Microsoft" button |
18	| `supabase/migrations/20260608100936_allow_azure_company_sso_signup.sql` | Trigger change — bypasses invitation check for Azure users |
19	
20	---
21	
22	## Path 2 — Authentik SSO (legacy company AD accounts)
23	
24	**Added 2026-05-09. Hidden from the login page on 2026-06-08, but not removed.**
25	
26	The legacy "Sign in with company account" button is hidden behind `SHOW_AUTHENTIK_SSO = false` in `src/pages/LoginPage.tsx` while Microsoft/Azure is the primary company SSO path. The backend Authentik flow remains in place for compatibility and can be re-enabled by toggling that constant.
27	
28	When this legacy path is re-enabled, users with a company Active Directory account log in via the "Sign in with company account" button on the login page. They are redirected to `auth.designflow.app`, authenticate with their AD credentials there, and are redirected back.
29	
30	### How it works
31	
32	1. **PKCE redirect** — the frontend generates a code verifier + challenge, stores the verifier in `sessionStorage`, and redirects the browser to Authentik's authorization endpoint.
33	2. **Authentik login** — user authenticates at `auth.designflow.app` with AD credentials.
34	3. **Code callback** — Authentik redirects to `https://dam.designflow.app/auth/callback` with an authorization code.
35	4. **Token exchange** — the `AuthCallbackPage` component exchanges the code at Authentik's token endpoint directly from the browser (public OIDC client, no secret needed).
36	5. **Session creation** — the `id_token` is sent to the `authenticate-with-authentik` Supabase edge function, which:
37	   - validates the token against Authentik's JWKS (`https://auth.designflow.app/application/o/popdam/jwks/`)
38	   - creates the Supabase user if this is their first login (with `app_metadata.provider = 'authentik'`)
39	   - returns a `token_hash`
40	6. **Supabase session** — the frontend calls `supabase.auth.verifyOtp({ token_hash, type: 'email' })` to exchange the hash for a live session.
41	
42	### Relevant files
43	
44	| File | Purpose |
45	|------|---------|
46	| `src/lib/authentik.ts` | PKCE helpers — redirect, code exchange |
47	| `src/pages/AuthCallbackPage.tsx` | Handles `/auth/callback`, runs steps 4–6 |
48	| `src/pages/LoginPage.tsx` | Contains the hidden "Sign in with company account" button |
49	| `supabase/functions/authenticate-with-authentik/` | Edge function — JWKS validation, user provisioning, token_hash |
50	| `supabase/migrations/20260509000000_authentik_invitation_bypass.sql` | Trigger change — bypasses invitation check for Authentik users |
51	
52	### New user provisioning
53	
54	The `handle_new_user` DB trigger normally blocks sign-ups that lack a pending invitation. When the edge function creates a new user it sets `app_metadata.provider = 'authentik'`, which the trigger detects and skips the invitation check for, auto-assigning the `user` role instead.
55	
56	Existing users (anyone who already has a Supabase account) are looked up by email and linked to the same account — no duplicate is created.
57	
58	### Authentik provider details
59	
60	| Setting | Value |
61	|---------|-------|
62	| Authentik app slug | `popdam` |
63	| Client ID | `q779goioDDC4P9QzTZM9GiO7SGUraIwPlMvFPb6j` |
64	| Client type | public (PKCE, no client secret) |
65	| Redirect URI | `https://dam.designflow.app/auth/callback` |
66	| OIDC discovery | `https://auth.designflow.app/application/o/popdam/.well-known/openid-configuration` |
67	
68	---
69	
70	## Path 3 — Google OAuth
71	
72	Users can sign in with a Google account via Supabase's built-in Google OAuth provider. Requires a pending invitation matching the Google account's email.
73	
74	---
75	
76	## Path 4 — Email / password
77	
78	Users can sign up or sign in with email + password. Sign-up requires a pending invitation for that email address. Password reset is handled by Supabase's magic-link flow.
79	
80	---
81	
82	## Session storage
83	
84	All paths produce a Supabase JWT. Sessions are stored in `localStorage` under the key `sb-popdam-auth-token` and auto-refreshed by the Supabase client. The `AuthProvider` in `src/hooks/useAuth.tsx` listens to `onAuthStateChange` and provides the session to the rest of the app.
85	
86	## Backend enforcement
87	
88	- **admin-api**: validates the Supabase JWT and checks the `user_roles` table for `admin` role
89	- **agent-api**: uses a separate `x-agent-key` — not tied to user auth
90	- **authenticate-with-authentik**: `verify_jwt = false` — it handles its own token validation via JWKS before creating the Supabase session

6	
7	## Project summary
8	
9	**PopDAM** is an internal Digital Asset Manager for licensed consumer-product art (Disney, Marvel, etc.). Source design files (PSD, AI) live on a Synology NAS. The system ingests them, generates thumbnails, uploads them to DigitalOcean Spaces, and gives the team a dark-mode web UI for browsing, searching, filtering, tagging, and managing artwork submissions.
10	
11	**PopSG** is a second mode served by the same codebase — a style-guide library for licensors (folder-based browsing, no SKUs or ERP). Same Docker image; hostname determines mode at runtime.
12	
13	**What matters:** assets get processed quickly, thumbnails appear, ERP codes resolve correctly, and style group assignments stay accurate. The team reviews and approves artwork against licensing deadlines.
14	
15	**Key moving parts:**
16	
17	| Component | Location | Platform |
18	|-----------|----------|----------|
19	| React web app | `src/` | Coolify (Docker, self-hosted VPS) |
20	| Supabase edge functions | `supabase/functions/` | Supabase (Deno) |
21	| PostgreSQL DB | `supabase/migrations/` | Supabase (hosted) |
22	| Cloud worker (AI tagging, ERP, rebuild) | `apps/worker/` | Railway (Node.js) |
23	| Bridge agent (NAS scanner + thumbnailer) | `apps/bridge-agent/` | Synology Docker |
24	| Windows render agent (Illustrator) | `apps/windows-agent/` | Windows VM (manual install) |
25	| Desktop helper (checkout/checkin) | `apps/popdam-helper/` | Electron, Mac + Windows |

79	## Shared-backend startup/shutdown hygiene
80	
81	Why this exists:
82	`popdam3`, `poppim-web`, and `popcrm-web` all depend on the same Supabase backend.
83	An unfinished migration or dirty canonical `u2giants/shared-db` checkout can block
84	unrelated app commits or, worse, ship a database change without the right preview
85	checks. Future AI sessions must keep shared-db work isolated and leave the
86	workspace clean enough for the next vibe-coding session.
87	
88	Startup checklist:
89	
90	1. Run `git status --short` in this repo before editing.
91	2. If the task may touch shared Supabase schema, RLS, API views/RPCs, generated
92	   database types, or cross-app data contracts, also run `git status --short` in
93	   `/worksp/shared-db` before editing.
94	3. Treat `shared-db/` inside this repo as a read-only mirror. Do not create or
95	   edit migrations there; use canonical `/worksp/shared-db`.
96	4. PopDAM-owned migrations under this repo's `supabase/migrations/` still follow
97	   this repo's migration discipline. Cross-app/shared-schema migrations belong in
98	   canonical `/worksp/shared-db`.
99	5. If `/worksp/shared-db` has untracked migrations or unrelated dirty files, stop
100	   and report them before creating new database work. Do not mix another
101	   session's shared-db changes into this app's commit.
102	6. Before creating a shared-db migration, create/switch to a dedicated
103	   `/worksp/shared-db` branch named for the database change. App repos commit to
104	   `main`; shared-db uses branch + PR.
105	
106	Shutdown checklist:
107	
108	1. Run `git status --short` in this repo and, if touched or inspected for backend
109	   work, in `/worksp/shared-db`.
110	2. No untracked shared-db migration may remain. Every shared-db migration must be
111	   committed on its own branch, stashed with a clear name, or removed if
112	   abandoned.
113	3. If shared-db work is incomplete, leave durable handoff text that names the
114	   branch/stash, migration file, preview/prod apply status, and the next exact
115	   action.
116	4. Final reports must separate app commits from shared-db status so the owner can
117	   keep vibe-coding without becoming the git janitor.

317	## Intentional quirks
318	
319	Use this exact shape for every new quirk:
320	
321	- **Looks like:** the misleading symptom.
322	- **Actually:** the true behavior.
323	- **Why:** the reason this behavior exists.
324	- **Do not change because:** the failure mode if someone "cleans it up."
325	
326	### Dual-mode (PopDAM / PopSG) via hostname detection
327	
328	**Looks like:** Two separate Supabase projects, two separate deployments.
329	**Actually:** One Docker image, one Coolify app, one Supabase project (`qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai`). `src/lib/app-mode.ts` reads `window.location.host` and returns `"popdam"` or `"popsg"`. `IS_POPSG` guards routes, UI panels, and page components throughout `App.tsx` and the components tree.
330	**Do not change because:** Splitting the mode into two builds or two containers would double the deployment surface for no functional gain.
331	**Local testing:** Add `?mode=popsg` to any localhost URL — stored in `sessionStorage` for the tab.
332	
333	### `asset_count` on `style_groups` is a cached field, not computed on read
334	
335	**Looks like:** `style_groups.asset_count` should always be up to date — just query `COUNT(*) FROM assets WHERE style_group_id = ...`.
336	**Actually:** `asset_count` is a denormalized cache. It is maintained by:
337	1. A statement-level trigger `trg_refresh_sg_counts_on_asset_change` (migration `20260515080654`) — fires on INSERT/DELETE of `assets` and on UPDATE when `is_deleted` or `style_group_id` changes.
338	2. A pg_cron job `nightly-reconcile-sg-asset-counts` (migration `20260531142011`) running at 03:45 UTC daily — calls `refresh_style_group_counts_batch(array_agg(id))` over all groups to catch any drift the trigger missed.
339	3. The `reconcile-style-group-stats` Railway worker op — on-demand full reconcile.
340	**Why cached:** Computing `COUNT(*)` with a join on every library page load at the style_groups level is prohibitively slow at scale.
341	**Drift can occur when:** A bulk asset delete bypasses the trigger (e.g., direct SQL via service role without triggering the transition table logic), or the trigger fires but the DB rolls back after the count update. Before the nightly cron was added (2026-05-31), pre-existing drift from before 2026-05-15 was never cleaned up — 17 style groups had stale counts including 2 with `asset_count=1` but zero actual assets.
342	**Do not change because:** Use `reconcile-style-group-stats` op or the nightly cron to fix drift; computing live would reintroduce expensive page loads.
343	
344	### `supabase-popsg/` directory is dead code
345	
346	**Looks like:** A separate Supabase project for PopSG with its own functions and workflow.
347	**Actually:** PopSG was originally on a separate project (`eeueczxhezfhyrhdmidg`). It was consolidated into the PopDAM project. The directory was never cleaned up.
348	**Do not change because:** `deploy-popsg-supabase.yml` is intentionally blocked; deploying from `supabase-popsg/` would target the old abandoned project.
349	
350	### `.ai` file sentinel pattern
351	
352	**Looks like:** The bridge agent is ignoring many `.ai` files for no obvious reason.
353	**Actually:** Adobe Illustrator can save `.ai` files without "Create PDF Compatible File" enabled. These files contain only a boilerplate compatibility-alert warning page — no actual artwork. The bridge agent detects them via `isAiWithoutPdfCompat()` in `apps/bridge-agent/src/thumbnailer.ts` (reads file header, then uses mupdf to check page text for the sentinel phrase "saved without PDF Content"). Detected files are permanently added to `scanner_ai_ignores` so they are never re-processed.
354	**Do not change because:** Without it, these files generate "compatibility alert" thumbnails that look like real artwork in the library.
355	
356	### ERP `product_category` cutoff date (2025-05-10)
357	
358	**Looks like:** Some ERP items have no `product_category` even though they have valid MG codes.
359	**Actually:** Before 2025-05-10, the MG01 field from the ERP API used single-letter codes with unstable meanings. After that date the letters reliably map to categories. The worker (`apps/worker/src/handlers/erp.ts`) only uses `mg_category` to set `product_category` when `erp_updated_at >= 2025-05-10`. Items before that date fall through to the AI prediction path. About 5,500 style groups with pre-cutoff ERP data have null `product_category` and need AI classification.
360	**Do not change because:** Items before the cutoff would get wrong categories applied automatically.
361	
362	### PLM production PO sync has two auth layers, and browser JWTs are not durable
363	
364	**Looks like:** `getProdOrderHeader` only needs the Cloud Run `Authorization` identity token and an `X-User-Authorization` value copied from the PLM web app.
365	**Actually:** Cloud Run auth and PLM app auth are separate. Cloud Run is handled server-side with `PROD_ORDER_GOOGLE_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_JSON` / service-account impersonation. The PLM app token (`X-User-Authorization`, stored as `PROD_ORDER_API_TOKEN_2`) is a short-lived browser JWT; one verified token expired on 2026-06-16 and later requests returned `403 Invalid Token`.
366	**Data shape gotcha:** the SKU is nested in `details[]` as `Item #` / `matchedItemNumber`; the production PO number is on the header as `Prod Reference #` / `Prod Order No`.
367	**Do not change because:** A copied browser token will keep expiring and breaking background sync. The durable fix is for the PLM/BFF developer to provide service-to-service auth: either trust the Cloud Run invoker service account, expose a client-credentials/token-refresh flow, or issue a long-lived read-only API token.
368	
369	### Bridge agent defers thumbnails to Windows Render Agent for certain files
370	
371	**Looks like:** Some `.ai` files get `thumbnail_error = "deferred_to_windows_agent"` even though the bridge agent could attempt to render them.
372	**Actually:** When `windows_render_mode = "primary"` or the `windows_render_policy` mode is set to `"shared"` with the file type in `shared_types`, the bridge agent intentionally skips local thumbnailing and queues a `render_queue` job for the Windows agent instead. The policy is set in `admin_config` and delivered via heartbeat response.
373	**Do not change because:** These are intentional deferrals, not errors. The Windows agent renders them via Illustrator (higher quality than the PDF-compat path).
374	
375	### PDF text backfill runs on the Windows agent, not the bridge agent
376	
377	**Looks like:** The bridge agent is the natural home for all NAS-side batch work, including the full-library PDF/.ai text extraction backfill.
378	**Actually:** `agent-api/handleHeartbeat()` routes `trigger_pdf_backfill` to the `windows-render` agent when a healthy Windows agent reports a **backfill-capable version (≥ 0.16.0)** — `windowsBackfillCapable` — and falls back to the `bridge` agent otherwise. This version/capability gate makes the cutover automatic and gap-free: the bridge keeps running the backfill until the Windows agent has self-updated to a build that actually contains the loop. The Windows agent (`apps/windows-agent/src/pdf-backfill.ts`) runs the same mupdf→OCR→AI extraction (reusing the `pdf-text-sampler` cascade) and shares the `claim-pdf-backfill-batch` / `complete-pdf-backfill-batch` endpoints, so all extraction CPU runs on the Windows VM instead of the Synology.
379	**Config-key gotcha:** the command only fires if `PDF_BACKFILL` is in the agent type's heartbeat config-key set (`getConfigKeysForAgent()` in `agent-api`). It must be present in `HEARTBEAT_CONFIG_KEYS_WINDOWS`, or the `windows-render` heartbeat never sees `configMap.PDF_BACKFILL` and the trigger is silently always-false.
380	**Handover gotcha:** the claim loop self-drives — once started it keeps claiming until `PDF_BACKFILL.status != "running"` or the queue is empty, independent of the heartbeat trigger. To hand the job from bridge → Windows cleanly, set `status=paused`, wait for the bridge to stop on its next claim, then `status=running`; otherwise both agents run concurrently (safe via `ON CONFLICT` dedupe, but wasteful).
381	**Status/UI gotcha:** the admin Backfill card reads `admin_config.PDF_BACKFILL` through `admin-api/get-pdf-backfill-status`, but the authoritative queue state is `count_pdf_backfill_remaining()`. Completion must be based on **remaining = 0**, not only `processed >= total`, because the initial total can become stale if files are sampled by another path while the job is running. The status route intentionally normalizes a stale `status="running"` row to `completed` when remaining is zero, so the UI shows a terminal result instead of silence or a forever-running state.
382	**Do not change because:** Reverting to bridge-only pushes heavy extraction onto the NAS CPU.
383	
384	### Style Guide Sources (`sku_files_used`) only come from licensing/tech-pack PDFs; resolution is fuzzy + continuous
385	
386	**Looks like:** `.ai` files and ordinary PDFs should populate a SKU's "Style Guide Sources," and unresolved entries are garbage to delete.
387	**Actually:** Only PDFs whose filename contains `licensing sheet`/`license sheet`/`tech pack`/`techpack` write `sku_files_used` (gate `is_style_guide_source_pdf()`, migration `20260610070731`). Resolution against the 214k-row `style_guide_files` is trigram-fuzzy (`resolve_sku_files_used_fuzzy`, nightly cron `resolve-sku-files-used-nightly` 04:00 UTC) and **quarantine-model — never auto-deletes/unlinks**. Full detail: `docs/POPSG.md` → "Style Guide Sources"; quirks #46–#48.
388	**Do not change because:** Do NOT bulk-delete unresolved `sku_files_used` rows that look like filenames — PopSG is not a comprehensive ground truth (a stale crawl can mark real files inactive; see quirk #46), so "no match" ≠ "garbage." Only categorical non-filenames (style-guide titles, a SKU used as its own filename) are safe to delete. Files-used live in PopSG `style_guide_files`, **not** PopDAM `assets` — don't reconcile against `assets`.
389	
390	### Sibling file scans need a 10-minute lease/expiry
391	
392	**Looks like:** `claimed` sibling scan requests should be treated exactly like `pending` requests until the Bridge Agent completes them.
393	**Actually:** The "Find Sibling Files" UI stores folder-scan jobs as `admin_config` rows named `sibling_scan_request_*`. The Bridge Agent claims a row, scans the NAS folder for sibling JPG/PNG/eligible PDF files, then reports completion through `complete-sibling-scan`. If the agent restarts or throws after claiming, the row can otherwise stay `claimed` forever and the UI will sit at "Waiting for Bridge Agent..." indefinitely.
394	**Do not change because:** `supabase/functions/_shared/admin-handlers/sibling-scan-handlers.ts` intentionally expires stale `claimed` rows after 10 minutes, and `supabase/functions/agent-api/index.ts` intentionally lets the Bridge Agent reclaim stale claims. `apps/bridge-agent/src/index.ts` also reports a failed scan when a per-request worker throws. Keep these together so a dead agent/request becomes a retryable failure instead of blocking future scans for the same folder.

409	
410	**Looks like:** Security hole — admin API doesn't verify JWTs at the gateway level.
411	**Actually:** CORS preflight (`OPTIONS`) carries no auth header; gateway-level JWT check rejects it. Verification happens inside the function. See `docs/KNOWN_QUIRKS.md` #4.
412	
413	### Style group rebuild `finalize_stats` calls `reconcile_style_group_stats_batch` in a loop
414	
415	**Looks like:** Should just call `run_full_reconcile_style_group_stats` once.
416	**Actually:** `run_full_reconcile_style_group_stats` has no `SET statement_timeout`, so after a full rebuild the DB-level role timeout kills it. The batched approach (100 groups/batch for counts, 25 for primaries) each has `SET statement_timeout = '120s'` and completes without hitting the limit.
417	**Do not change because:** "Start Fresh" rebuild reliably times out on "Compute counts" when there are many groups.
418	
419	### `trg_sync_primary_on_thumbnail` fires on INSERT **and** UPDATE
420	
421	**Looks like:** Overkill — why would an INSERT need to sync a cover?
422	**Actually:** The bridge agent sets `thumbnail_url` at insert time (single DB write). If the trigger only fired on UPDATE (which it did before migration `20260529132758`), those assets never triggered the sync, leaving `primary_asset_id = null`. A backfill in that migration fixed 482 affected groups.
423	**Do not change because:** Reverting to UPDATE-only would silently break cover assignment for any asset inserted with a thumbnail already set.
424	
425	### Railway worker deploys on every push to `main`
426	
427	**Looks like:** Wasteful — most pushes don't touch `apps/worker/`.
428	**Actually:** Railway doesn't support path filters. Every push triggers a Railway rebuild regardless of which files changed. This is a Railway platform constraint, not a bug.
429	
430	### Two separate `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` locations
431	
432	**Looks like:** Duplication or confusion.
433	**Actually:** `admin_config.OPENROUTER_API_KEY` feeds bridge/windows agents via heartbeat response. Railway env `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` feeds the Railway worker directly. Setting one does not set the other.
434	
435	### `.mcp.json` carries no secrets — MCP tokens come from 1Password (do NOT re-hardcode)
436	
437	**Looks like:** `devops-mcp`/`synology-monitor` in the root `.mcp.json` have `Bearer ${DEVOPS_MCP_TOKEN}` / `${NAS_MCP_TOKEN}` placeholders that "should" hold the actual token.
438	**Actually:** The tokens are injected from 1Password (`vibe_coding/designflow-mcp`) — on the VPS via a `~/.bashrc` `op read` block, elsewhere via `op run`. The old hardcoded tokens were exposed in git history and had to be rotated (2026-06-22).
439	**Do not change because:** pasting real tokens back into `.mcp.json` commits them to git and re-exposes them. Full model + rotation steps: `docs/MCP_SERVERS.md`.
440	
441	### `src/integrations/supabase/client.ts` is a one-line re-export
442	
443	**Looks like:** Should create a Supabase client.
444	**Actually:** Re-exports from `external-supabase.ts` so that Lovable overwrites don't break production. See `docs/KNOWN_QUIRKS.md` #2.
445	
446	### Supabase credentials hardcoded in `src/lib/app-mode.ts`
447	
448	**Looks like:** Security anti-pattern.
449	**Actually:** The anon key is a publishable key (like a Firebase web API key); the service role key is never hardcoded. Lovable overwrites `.env` on every deploy, so env vars can't be trusted. See `docs/KNOWN_QUIRKS.md` #1.
450	**Do not change because:** Moving these to env vars would make all queries silently route to the empty Lovable-provisioned project.
451	
452	### Helper storage provider is per-machine/region, not a global flag
453	
454	**Looks like:** `admin_config.HELPER_SEAFILE_PREFERRED` should globally switch all designers to Seafile.
455	**Actually:** Transport is chosen **per machine by region** — Brazil (WFH) → Seafile/SeaDrive, USA → Synology `edgesynology1` over SMB. The Helper's local `config.preferredProvider` is the real lever (set at install); `HELPER_SEAFILE_LIBRARIES` + `HELPER_SYNOLOGY_FALLBACK_ALLOWED` flow from `admin_config` via `helper-api /config`. Brazil keeps a Synology fallback over Tailscale SMB. A library is matched by **longest path-prefix** on `relative_path` (a PopDAM root can hold multiple Seafile libraries as subfolders).
456	**Do not change because:** A single global flag breaks the region split; see `docs/SEAFILE_INTEGRATION.md`.
457	
458	### SeaDrive installer is self-hosted and auto-mirrored by the worker
459	
460	**Looks like:** The Downloads page should just link seafile.com for the SeaDrive client.
461	**Actually:** The Railway worker's `seadrive-mirror` handler runs weekly from `tick()`, scrapes the official SeaDrive download page, and mirrors the latest `.pkg`/`.msi` into the `popdam` Spaces bucket, recording `admin_config.SEADRIVE_LATEST` ({version, mac_url, win_url, mirrored, checked_at}). The Downloads page reads that and serves the pinned hosted version (fallback: official URLs). Spaces creds come from `admin_config.DO_SPACES_*` — the worker has no Spaces env var.
462	**Do not change because:** Removing the mirror reverts to an uncontrolled third-party download; the LRU cache/pinning is SeaDrive-native (not our code).
463	
464	### Seafile check-ins park in `verifying` status before completing
465	
466	**Looks like:** Check-in should immediately mark the checkout `complete` once the helper's upload returns, the same as a Synology direct upload.
467	**Actually:** For Seafile-sourced check-ins (`source_provider = 'seafile'`), the file travels designer → Seafile server → Synology NAS (extra hop). The helper's upload returning proves only that the file arrived on Seafile, not that it landed intact on the Synology. `helper-api/complete-checkin` parks these checkouts in `status: 'verifying'` (lock still held) instead of `complete`. The bridge agent (running on the Synology) claims pending verifications via `claim-checkin-verifications`, stat-checks the on-disk file for size match, then computes a quick-hash (SHA-256 of first 64 KB + last 64 KB + size — a ~128 KB read). On match it calls `report-checkin-verification` and the checkout advances to `complete`. Synology direct uploads bypass this entirely and complete immediately as before.
468	**Feature flag:** gated by `admin_config.CHECKIN_VERIFICATION_ENABLED` (read in `complete-checkin`). **Activated 2026-06-09.** When off/absent, Seafile check-ins complete immediately like before — set it to `false` for instant rollback, no redeploy. It was shipped *dark* first because helper-api and the bridge agent deploy via different pipelines; activating before the verifying-capable agent (≥ v1.16.0) is live would hang check-ins with nothing to confirm them.
469	**Why:** Releasing the lock before the file has synced defeats the checkout/check-in guarantee — a second designer could check out and overwrite a partially-synced file.
470	**Do not change because:** `verifying` is included in the `asset_checkouts_one_active_per_asset` partial unique index (same as `active`), so the lock is held throughout. Removing the step silently re-introduces a race condition for WFH Brazil check-ins.
471	**Timing / deadlines:** T1 = 30 min (`verify_deadline_at`) — flag surfaced to designer + admin, re-drive triggered. T2 = 2 hours (`verify_resolve_at`) — auto-resolve releases the lock into `error` with diagnostics. Both deadlines freeze when the bridge agent is offline (detected by a gap > 3 heartbeats in `verify_last_attempt_at`); see `handleReportCheckinVerification` in `supabase/functions/agent-api/index.ts`.
472	**Code:** `supabase/functions/helper-api/index.ts` (complete-checkin Seafile branch), `supabase/functions/agent-api/index.ts` (claim-checkin-verifications / report-checkin-verification), `apps/bridge-agent/src/checkin-verifier.ts`, migration `20260609120000_asset_checkouts_receipt_verification.sql`.
473	
474	---
475	
476	### Agent reported `version` can lie — the admin panel trusts `build_sha`, not the version string
477	
478	**Looks like:** The Settings → Bridge Agents "Up to date ✅" badge means the agent is running the latest published code.
479	**Actually:** The agent reports three identity fields. `version` is read from `package.json` at **runtime** (a mutable, human-edited string). `image_tag` and `build_sha` identify the running image and — **as of bridge v1.16.4** — are read from an **immutable `/app/build-info.json` baked into the image** (`readBuildInfo()` in `apps/bridge-agent/src/index.ts`), **not** from env vars. This file-based source matters: the self-updater's `recreateViaDockerRun` clones the previous container's entire env as explicit `-e` flags, which **used to override the env-based `POPDAM_BUILD_SHA`/`POPDAM_IMAGE_TAG`** and freeze them at the first image's values even after a *successful* update (see Incidents 2026-06-21 + `docs/KNOWN_QUIRKS.md` #26). A file in the image layer can't be overridden by env-cloning, so the reported `build_sha` now always matches the running image. The panel compares the agent's `build_sha` against `BRIDGE_LATEST_BUILD.sha` (returned by admin-api `get-latest-agent-build`). "Up to date" is sha-based; a version-matches-but-build-differs state renders a red **"Build mismatch"** warning with the recovery command.
480	**Why:** On 2026-06-09 the panel showed v1.16.0 "up to date" while the NAS container ran v1.9.6 — the version string hid a failed update; `build_sha` exposed it. But the env-based `build_sha` then produced **false** "Build mismatch" alerts on every later (successful) update because the env-clone froze it — root-caused and fixed 2026-06-21 by moving build identity to the immutable file. Env vars remain only as a pre-1.16.4 / dev fallback.
481	**Do not change because:** The self-updater is fragile (see `docs/KNOWN_QUIRKS.md` #26 — ~50 iterations to stabilize) and was deliberately left untouched; the fix went into the build (file) + reader, not the recreate logic. **Do not "simplify" `readBuildInfo()` back to `process.env.POPDAM_BUILD_SHA`** — that re-introduces the env-clone freeze and the false alerts. Code: `apps/bridge-agent/{Dockerfile,src/index.ts}`, `src/pages/SettingsPage.tsx` (`AgentStatusSection`), `supabase/functions/admin-api/index.ts` (`handleGetLatestAgentBuild`).
482	
483	---
484	
485	### `stage` is not `workflow_status` (path-derived attributes)

1	# AGENTS.md — popcrm-web
2	
3	Canonical operating guide and documentation router for **popcrm-web**. Read this
4	first; load other docs only when the task needs them (see the Documentation map).
5	
6	## Project summary
7	
8	`popcrm-web` is the **POP CRM frontend** — a Vite/React/TypeScript single-page app
9	served by nginx, used by internal POP Creations staff to run customer, sales,
10	licensing and product-development workflows: accounts, contacts, the program
11	pipeline, Outlook-ingested email routing, Fireflies meeting notes, notes, tasks,
12	licensor approvals, and AI-model settings.
13	
14	It **stores no data of its own** — every read/write goes through the shared
15	Supabase project (`https://qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai.supabase.co`). The CRM backend is
16	a Supabase/Postgres port of the earlier Twenty→Directus CRM data model. The
17	outcome that matters: a fast, dense operations console over that shared data.
18	
19	- Production: `https://crm.designflow.app`
20	- Preview alias: `https://crm-dev.designflow.app`
21	- Backend (Supabase): `https://qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai.supabase.co`
22	- Fireflies webhook/health: `https://crm-fireflies.designflow.app`
23	- Sibling frontends (separate repos): `poppim-web` (PIM), `popdam-web` (DAM)
24	
25	## Multi-model AI note
26	
27	There is no universal ignore-file standard across AI coding tools.
28	
29	`.claudeignore` works for Claude Code.
30	
31	When using any other AI tool, paste this file as your first message and follow the instructions in the "What to ignore" section.
32	
33	## Documentation map: what to read for each task
34	
35	Always start with:
36	
37	- `AGENTS.md`
38	
39	Then load additional docs only when relevant:
40	
41	| Task / question | Read these docs | Usually do not need |
42	|---|---|---|
43	| Quick repo orientation | `README.md`, `AGENTS.md` | Deep docs under `docs/`, `frontend_imp.md` |
44	| Modify app behavior or project-owned code | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/architecture.md` if data flow/shape changes | `docs/deployment.md` unless deploy behavior changes |
45	| Add/change config, env vars, or runtime settings | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/configuration.md`, `docs/deployment.md` if prod/runtime is affected | Unrelated architecture docs |
46	| Change local setup, dev/test/lint scripts, or tooling | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/development.md`, `package.json`, `eslint.config.js` | `docs/deployment.md` unless CI/CD changes |
47	| Change deployment, Docker, CI/CD, hosting, release, or rollback | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/deployment.md`, `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`, `Dockerfile`, `nginx.conf` | Local-only dev docs |
48	| Change data shape, Supabase fields, queries, views, RPCs, or identifiers | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, `src/lib/types.ts`, `src/lib/database.types.ts`, `src/features/crm/api.ts`, `shared-db/supabase/migrations/` | Deployment docs unless rollout changes |
49	| Investigate bugs or incidents | `AGENTS.md` (Critical incidents), area-relevant source, `HANDOFF.md` if present | Unrelated docs |
50	| Continue unfinished work | `AGENTS.md`, `HANDOFF.md` (if present) and the docs it names | Docs outside the handoff scope |
51	| Understand the redesign rationale (charts, tokens, layout) | `frontend_imp.md` (historical plan — fully implemented) | Everything else |
52	| Claude Code session | `CLAUDE.md`, then `AGENTS.md` | Other docs unless the task requires them |
53	| Documentation-only cleanup | `AGENTS.md`, `README.md`, affected `docs/`, ignore files | Source files except to verify accuracy |
54	| Pull secrets from 1Password (MCP server or `op` CLI) | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/1password.md` | Unrelated architecture/deploy docs |
55	
56	Notes:
57	- `HANDOFF.md` is **absent** when there is no unfinished work. If it exists, it is
58	  required reading for continuation tasks.
59	- `frontend_imp.md` is a large background design plan — now fully implemented. It is in
60	  `.claudeignore`/`.cursorignore` to keep it out of routine AI context.
61	- `design_handoff_popcrm_elevation/` is a historical design-spec directory — also ignored.
62	- Shared non-code infrastructure/server standards live in
63	  [`u2giants/albert-standards/infrastructure`](https://github.com/u2giants/albert-standards/tree/main/infrastructure).
64	  When deployment, domains, runtime ownership, server dependencies, break-glass
65	  runbooks, or infrastructure decisions change for this app, update that repo too.
66	
67	## Shared-backend startup/shutdown hygiene
68	
69	Why this exists:
70	`popcrm-web`, `poppim-web`, and `popdam3` all depend on the same Supabase backend.
71	An unfinished migration or dirty canonical `u2giants/shared-db` checkout can block
72	unrelated app commits or, worse, ship a database change without the right preview
73	checks. Future AI sessions must keep shared-db work isolated and leave the
74	workspace clean enough for the next vibe-coding session.
75	
76	Startup checklist:
77	
78	1. Run `git status --short` in this repo before editing.
79	2. If the task may touch Supabase schema, RLS, API views/RPCs, generated database
80	   types, or cross-app data contracts, also run `git status --short` in
81	   `/worksp/shared-db` before editing.
82	3. Treat `shared-db/` inside this repo as a read-only mirror. Do not create or
83	   edit migrations there; use canonical `/worksp/shared-db`.
84	4. If `/worksp/shared-db` has untracked migrations or unrelated dirty files, stop
85	   and report them before creating new database work. Do not mix another
86	   session's shared-db changes into this app's commit.
87	5. Before creating a shared-db migration, create/switch to a dedicated
88	   `/worksp/shared-db` branch named for the database change. App repos commit to
89	   `main`; shared-db uses branch + PR.
90	
91	Shutdown checklist:
92	
93	1. Run `git status --short` in this repo and, if touched or inspected for backend
94	   work, in `/worksp/shared-db`.
95	2. No untracked shared-db migration may remain. Every shared-db migration must be
96	   committed on its own branch, stashed with a clear name, or removed if
97	   abandoned.
98	3. If shared-db work is incomplete, leave durable handoff text that names the
99	   branch/stash, migration file, preview/prod apply status, and the next exact
100	   action.
101	4. Final reports must separate app commits from shared-db status so the owner can
102	   keep vibe-coding without becoming the git janitor.
103	
104	## Repository structure
105	
106	Project-owned application code:
107	
108	- `src/app/` — shell + router: `AppLayout.tsx`, `routes.tsx`, `navigation.ts`
109	- `src/components/app/` — shared building blocks: `DataTable`, `DetailDrawer`,
110	  `MetricCard`, `PageToolbar`, `AppPage`, `AppSidebar`, `AppHeader`, `Combobox`,
111	  `CommandSearch`, `FilterSelect`, `StatusBadge`, `states.tsx`
112	- `src/features/crm/` — CRM domain:
113	  - `CrmDataContext.tsx` — loads all collections once; exposes state, refresh, stats
114	  - `api.ts` — Supabase view/RPC/table reads and writes · `constants.ts` · `format.ts` · `useRecordSelection.ts`
115	  - `pages/` — one module per route (Overview, Pipeline, Accounts, Contacts,
116	    EmailRouting, Meetings, Notes, Tasks, Approvals, Settings) + `_shared.ts`
117	  - `components/` — domain drawers (Email/Opportunity/Account/Contact/Task/Note/
118	    Meeting/Approval) + `CrmStatusBadge`, `RelationLabel`
119	- `src/auth/auth.tsx` — Supabase auth/profile state · `src/pages/LoginPage.tsx`
120	- `src/lib/` — `supabase.ts` (client), `database.types.ts` (generated schema), `types.ts` (frontend domain types), `utils.ts`
121	- `src/App.tsx`, `src/main.tsx`, `src/index.css` (shared OKLCH design tokens)
122	
123	Generated-style primitives (shadcn): `src/components/ui/*` — see Core modification inventory.
124	
125	Build / config / deploy:
126	
127	- `Dockerfile`, `nginx.conf`, `.github/workflows/deploy.yml`
128	- `vite.config.ts`, `tsconfig*.json`, `eslint.config.js`, `components.json`, `package.json`
129	
130	Docs: `README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, `docs/*`, `frontend_imp.md`
131	
132	Build artifacts / ignored: `dist/`, `node_modules/` (see What to ignore).
133	
134	Shared Supabase migrations live under `shared-db/supabase/migrations/`; legacy
135	Directus code under `/worksp/directus` is rollback/reference context only.
136	
137	## Prime Directive: custom-code boundary
138	
139	Our custom code lives here:
140	
141	- `src/app/`
142	- `src/components/app/`
143	- `src/features/`
144	- `src/auth/`, `src/pages/`, `src/lib/`
145	- `docs/`
146	- `.github/workflows/`
147	- root config: `Dockerfile`, `nginx.conf`, `vite.config.ts`, `tsconfig*.json`, `eslint.config.js`, `package.json`
148	
149	Everything else requires justification before touching. In particular,
150	`src/components/ui/*` are shadcn-style primitives (see below).

1	# Architecture — popcrm-web
2	
3	`popcrm-web` is a static React SPA for POP CRM.
4	
5	```txt
6	Browser
7	  -> https://crm.designflow.app
8	  -> popcrm-web nginx container
9	  -> React/Vite SPA
10	  -> Supabase JS client
11	  -> https://qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai.supabase.co
12	  -> shared Supabase/Postgres backend
13	```
14	
15	Fireflies integration is handled by a separate worker endpoint:
16	
17	```txt
18	Fireflies
19	  -> https://crm-fireflies.designflow.app/s/fireflies-webhook
20	  -> popcrm-fireflies container
21	  -> Supabase CRM tables
22	```
23	
24	Important operational note: the Fireflies `/health` endpoint only proves the
25	webhook server is up. It does not prove Fireflies is delivering webhook events.
26	On 2026-06-14, the CRM backend had 27 `crm_meeting_note` rows with latest meeting
27	date `2026-04-14`, while the Fireflies API listed newer transcripts through
28	`2026-06-11` and worker/proxy logs showed no recent webhook deliveries. Verify
29	stale ingestion from CRM row dates, worker/proxy logs, and the Fireflies
30	dashboard webhook configuration.
31	
32	The frontend has no database and stores no CRM data locally. It uses Supabase
33	Auth in the browser, then loads the user's CRM profile with
34	`api.current_user_profile()`.
35	
36	## Data loading
37	
38	Data loading is page-scoped through TanStack Query hooks in
39	`src/features/crm/queries.ts`. The app no longer bootstraps every CRM collection
40	on mount. Until a screen has real server-side pagination/search/filtering plus
41	true aggregate counts, list queries must load the full relevant dataset through
42	the paged helpers in `api.ts`. A 2026-06-22 TanStack Query refactor briefly used
43	hard-coded 50/100/300 row limits and made production screens look empty even
44	though data was intact. Page-scoped is fine; arbitrary partial slices are not.
45	High-change surfaces such as Email Routing, Tasks, Meetings, and Notes also use
46	conservative background refetch intervals.
47	
48	Reads in `src/features/crm/api.ts` go through browser-safe `api.crm_*` views.
49	Core account/contact writes use guarded RPCs (`api.crm_update_account`,
50	`api.crm_update_contact`); CRM-owned rows write to `crm.*` tables or narrower RPCs.
51	Write operations use mutation hooks in `queries.ts`, with optimistic query-cache
52	updates where the UI already had instant behavior. Related queries are invalidated
53	after writes so joined view rows reconcile with Supabase.
54	
55	Contact writes have one extra wrinkle: CRM relationship attributes
56	(`department`, `contact_type`, `scope`) live on `core.contact_company`, not
57	`core.contact`. The frontend must send the current account when editing those
58	fields so `api.crm_update_contact` can target the correct relationship row. The
59	2026-06-23 migration
60	`shared-db/supabase/migrations/20260623024500_crm_update_contact_clear_relationship_fields.sql`
61	adds explicit `p_clear_*` flags because `null` alone is ambiguous in RPC calls
62	and the old `coalesce` semantics could not clear values. Until that migration is
63	confirmed applied everywhere, `updateBuyer` keeps a fallback call using the old
64	RPC argument shape.
65	
66	Realtime frontend scaffolding lives in `src/features/crm/realtime.ts`. It listens
67	for base-table changes, debounces bursty tables, and invalidates the relevant
68	query keys so the app refetches browser-safe `api.crm_*` views instead of trying
69	to render raw realtime payloads. Supabase publication/RLS enablement is shared-db
70	work and must follow `shared-db/AGENTS.md`.
71	
72	Contacts are intentionally special after the 2026-06-22 incidents. The frontend
73	uses `api.crm_contact_segment_list` for the Cust Contacts, Dept. Contacts, and
74	Triage tabs, and `api.crm_contact_segment_counts` for tab counts. The All tab
75	uses the same segmented view without a segment filter, but it is lazy-loaded only
76	after the user opens All. Do not reintroduce PostgREST `order('name')` or
77	server-side `company_customer_status` filters for contact reads: the previous
78	`security_invoker` view plus derived-field ordering/filtering timed out on the
79	third paged browser request and made Contacts show all zeroes. The durable
80	database fix is
81	`shared-db/supabase/migrations/20260622043000_crm_contact_segments.sql`, which
82	preserves `api.crm_contact_list`, adds the explicit `app.has_app_access('crm')`
83	guard, and exposes the server-computed segment/count contracts.

3	Canonical operating guide for **poppim-web**. Read this first; it routes you to everything else.

5	## 1. Project summary
6	`poppim-web` is the **PIM (product/project management) frontend** for POP Creations — a React single-page app that is the human UI for the product-development pipeline, replacing the ClickUp board. **Users:** internal staff (designers, sales, licensing, management). It stores **no data of its own**; every read/write goes through the **shared Supabase.com backend** at `https://qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai.supabase.co` (shared schema repo: `u2giants/shared-db`). The outcome that matters: a fast, tailored Kanban + task-detail app on the company's shared "super-app" database, so PIM data interlinks with CRM/DAM. Sibling frontends (separate repos): `popcmr-web` (CRM), `popdam-web` (DAM).
7	
8	**Live in production:** `https://pm.designflow.app` (the permanent human URL). Preview aliases `pm-dev` and `pm-ci` serve the same Coolify service. Deploy via `git push main` — see §13 and `docs/cicd.md`.
9	
10	**Backend direction:** this app is now on the shared **Supabase.com** backend (one DB for PM/CRM/DAM/PLM). For schema, RLS, realtime, migration, or cross-app changes, read `shared-db/AGENTS.md` first and update the canonical `u2giants/shared-db` repo; this repo should not carry app-only permanent DDL.

49	## Shared-backend startup/shutdown hygiene
50	
51	Why this exists:
52	`poppim-web`, `popcrm-web`, and `popdam3` all depend on the same Supabase backend.
53	An unfinished migration or dirty canonical `u2giants/shared-db` checkout can block
54	unrelated app commits or, worse, ship a database change without the right preview
55	checks. Future AI sessions must keep shared-db work isolated and leave the
56	workspace clean enough for the next vibe-coding session.
57	
58	Startup checklist:
59	
60	1. Run `git status --short` in this repo before editing.
61	2. If the task may touch Supabase schema, RLS, API views/RPCs, generated database
62	   types, or cross-app data contracts, also run `git status --short` in
63	   `/worksp/shared-db` before editing.
64	3. Treat `shared-db/` inside this repo as a read-only mirror. Do not create or
65	   edit migrations there; use canonical `/worksp/shared-db`.
66	4. If `/worksp/shared-db` has untracked migrations or unrelated dirty files, stop
67	   and report them before creating new database work. Do not mix another
68	   session's shared-db changes into this app's commit.
69	5. Before creating a shared-db migration, create/switch to a dedicated
70	   `/worksp/shared-db` branch named for the database change. App repos commit to
71	   `main`; shared-db uses branch + PR.
72	
73	Shutdown checklist:
74	
75	1. Run `git status --short` in this repo and, if touched or inspected for backend
76	   work, in `/worksp/shared-db`.
77	2. No untracked shared-db migration may remain. Every shared-db migration must be
78	   committed on its own branch, stashed with a clear name, or removed if
79	   abandoned.
80	3. If shared-db work is incomplete, leave durable handoff text that names the
81	   branch/stash, migration file, preview/prod apply status, and the next exact
82	   action.
83	4. Final reports must separate app commits from shared-db status so the owner can
84	   keep vibe-coding without becoming the git janitor.
